title: ROC and Precision-Recall Curves
concept: ROC and PR curves evaluate model performance across all thresholds, showing trade-offs between metrics.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main benefit of analyzing the off-diagonal cells in a confusion matrix?

options.text: They pinpoint which specific classes the model is confusing.
They display the exact training loss.

exercises.explain: Correct! Off-diagonal cells reveal systematic errors between pairs of classes.

cards.title: Scanning the spectrum
cards.body: Setting a single decision threshold is like looking at a photo with only one contrast level. To truly understand model capacity, we need to scan the threshold from 0 to 1, graphing how the model behaves at every level. This is what curves do.

cards.title: ROC vs. PR Curves
cards.body:
ROC (Receiver Operating Characteristic) Curve: Plots True Positive Rate vs. False Positive Rate. Summarized by AUC (Area Under Curve).
PR (Precision-Recall) Curve: Plots Precision vs. Recall. Preferred for highly imbalanced datasets because it ignores True Negatives, focusing on the rare class.

cards.title: Tuning a radio dial
cards.body: Think of turning a radio tuner dial. You sweep across frequencies to find the channel. The curve graphs the signal quality (precision) vs. the range of stations you capture (recall) as you adjust the dial.

cards.title: PR vs. ROC in imbalance
cards.body: In a dataset with 9,990 clean emails and 10 spam emails, a model that predicts spam for 100 clean emails achieves a very low False Positive Rate of 1%, making the ROC curve look perfect (AUC = 0.99). But the PR curve drops to 9% precision, exposing the high rate of false alarms.

cards.title: AUC-ROC is not the universal standard
cards.body: You might think a high AUC-ROC score (like 0.98) always means your model is excellent. In reality, if your dataset is highly imbalanced, a model can have a high ROC-AUC while producing mostly false alarms. You should use AUC-PR instead.

left.text:
Plots TPR vs. FPR
Best for balanced datasets
Affected by high True Negatives

right.text:
Plots Precision vs. Recall
Best for rare target classes
Ignores True Negatives

exercises.prompt: When is a Precision-Recall (PR) curve preferred over an ROC curve?
exercises.explain: PR curves focus on precision and recall, isolating minority class performance without being skewed by large numbers of true negatives.

options.text: When the dataset is highly imbalanced and the minority class is the focus
When the model is trained in less than 5 epochs
When we want to evaluate model latency
When there are only negative samples in the dataset

exercises.prompt: True or false: The Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC-ROC) ranges from 0.0 to 1.0, where 0.5 represents a model performing at random guessing level.
exercises.explain: True. An AUC of 0.5 means the model has no discriminative ability, performing no better than a coin flip.
An AUC score of 1.0 represents a perfect model with zero errors across all thresholds.
exercises.sentence: The maximum possible Area Under the Curve (AUC) value is ___.

exercises.prompt: Which of these metrics evaluates overall threshold-independent model performance?
exercises.explain: AUC aggregates performance across all possible decision thresholds, summarizing the entire curve.

exercises.prompt: You are building a credit card fraud detection system. 99.9% of transactions are legitimate. You show your manager a model with an AUC-ROC of 0.99. However, the model blocks 500 clean accounts for every 1 true fraud case. What metric did you ignore, and what curve should you use?

options.text: Precision was ignored due to massive True Negatives inflating the ROC. Use a Precision-Recall (PR) curve instead.
Accuracy was ignored. Use simple accuracy instead.
Latency was ignored. Use a speed curve instead.
No metrics were ignored; the manager is incorrect.

exercises.explain: Exactly! The massive volume of clean transactions (True Negatives) made the False Positive Rate look tiny, inflating the ROC curve. The PR curve focuses on Precision, exposing the fact that the positive predictions are mostly false alarms (Precision = 1/501 ≈ 0.2%). Next, let's look at choosing the right metric for a business goal!
